meanings.definition: A distinctive feature or characteristic; a mark of excellence or quality.
meanings.example: Precision of language and economy of expression were the hallmarks of her academic writing.

meanings.definition: An official mark stamped on articles of gold, silver, or platinum to certify their purity.
meanings.example: The hallmark confirmed that the silver cutlery was of the required standard and had been assayed in London.

meanings.definition: To hinder or impede the progress or movement of something; to obstruct or restrict.
meanings.example: The heavy snowfall hampered rescue operations, limiting the teams to daylight hours in the worst-affected areas.

meanings.definition: Acutely distressing; intensely painful or disturbing to experience or witness.
meanings.example: The survivors gave harrowing testimony about conditions in the detention facility during the tribunal hearing.

meanings.definition: Of the same kind; uniform in composition or character throughout.
meanings.example: The study found that the most innovative companies were typically less homogeneous in their leadership teams.

meanings.definition: In chemistry or mathematics, of the same degree or having a consistent mathematical property throughout.
meanings.example: The mixture was not truly homogeneous; under the microscope, clear phase separation could be detected.

meanings.definition: A mode of behaviour or way of thought peculiar to an individual; a distinctive personal or group characteristic.
meanings.example: One of his charming idiosyncrasies was his habit of writing the date in three different calendrical systems at the top of every document.

meanings.definition: Public shame or disgrace; the loss of honour, reputation, or self-respect.
meanings.example: The minister resigned in ignominy after the revelation that he had misrepresented his academic qualifications.

meanings.definition: Based on illusion; not real; deceptive in appearance or impression.
meanings.example: The apparent prosperity of the economic boom years proved illusory once debt obligations began to crystallise.

meanings.definition: About to happen; likely to occur very soon.
meanings.example: With the deadline imminent and key members still uncontactable, the project manager called an emergency strategy session.

meanings.definition: Implied though not plainly expressed; understood without being stated directly.
meanings.example: There was an implicit understanding between the two parties that certain matters would not be raised in public.

meanings.definition: With no qualification or question; absolute.
meanings.example: The general demanded implicit obedience from the troops, allowing no deviation from orders.

meanings.definition: Unable to express oneself clearly or fluently in speech; poorly expressed.
meanings.example: He was inarticulate with grief, able only to shake his head when asked how he was coping.

meanings.definition: Not expressed in words; not articulated or voiced.
meanings.example: The film captured the inarticulate longing of a generation that had been given abundance but not purpose.

meanings.definition: Not able to be corrected, improved, or reformed; habitual and unrepentant.
meanings.example: Despite numerous formal warnings, the incorrigible troublemaker continued to disrupt every faculty meeting.

meanings.definition: To make someone appear guilty of a crime or wrongdoing; to furnish evidence against.
meanings.example: The emails released by the investigators appeared to incriminate two senior officials in the tender manipulation scheme.

meanings.definition: Making marks that cannot be removed; not able to be erased.
meanings.example: The artist worked with indelible ink, knowing that every line would be permanent and correction impossible.

meanings.definition: Not able to be forgotten or removed from the mind; enduring.
meanings.example: The experience left an indelible impression on all who witnessed it, shaping their professional conduct for decades.

meanings.definition: To suggest or hint at something unpleasant in an indirect and usually devious way.
meanings.example: Rather than accusing him directly, she insinuated that certain gaps in his CV deserved explanation.

meanings.definition: To gradually introduce something or manoeuvre oneself into a position in a subtle or manipulative way.
meanings.example: He had managed to insinuate himself into the inner circle of the organisation through years of careful networking.

meanings.definition: To examine one's own thoughts and feelings; to engage in self-examination.
meanings.example: The therapist encouraged her to introspect more deeply rather than immediately seeking external validation for her decisions.

meanings.definition: To put someone or something into a situation in which there is a danger of loss, harm, or failure.
meanings.example: Any further breach of conduct would jeopardise not only his career but the entire department's credibility.

meanings.definition: The state of being legally responsible for something; a legal obligation.
meanings.example: The company accepted liability for the environmental damage and agreed to fund a restoration programme.

meanings.definition: A thing for which someone is responsible, especially a debt or financial obligation.
meanings.example: The hidden liabilities disclosed in the due diligence report significantly reduced the acquisition price.

meanings.definition: A person or thing whose presence or involvement causes a disadvantage.
meanings.example: By the third act, the protagonist's impulsiveness had become a liability that alienated every potential ally.

meanings.definition: A general feeling of discomfort, unease, or indisposition, often with no precise cause; a lack of energy or enthusiasm affecting a group or institution.
meanings.example: A deep cultural malaise had settled over the organisation, with experienced staff departing at an accelerating rate.

meanings.definition: To treat someone as if they are not important; to place or keep someone in a powerless or unimportant position in society.
meanings.example: The new policy risked further marginalising communities that were already underrepresented in public services.

meanings.definition: Excessive interest in or admiration of oneself and one's physical appearance; vanity and self-love.
meanings.example: The proliferation of social media has been linked by some researchers to rising levels of narcissism among young adults.

meanings.definition: In psychology, a personality disorder characterised by a grandiose sense of self-importance, a need for admiration, and a lack of empathy.
meanings.example: Narcissistic personality disorder is considered one of the more resistant conditions to conventional psychotherapy.

meanings.definition: Not aware of or concerned about what is happening around one; unaware or unmindful.
meanings.example: She continued working on the manuscript, apparently oblivious to the heated argument unfolding in the next room.

meanings.definition: To move back and forth between two points; to vary between extremes.
meanings.example: The pendulum oscillated with precise regularity, losing just a fraction of a second per day.

meanings.definition: To keep changing between opinions, states, or conditions; to waver.
meanings.example: Public opinion on the issue continued to oscillate between reform and preservation, reflecting genuine uncertainty about the right approach.

meanings.definition: A simple story used to illustrate a moral or spiritual lesson; a brief allegorical narrative.
meanings.example: The parable of the prodigal son explores themes of forgiveness, repentance, and the nature of parental love.

meanings.definition: Strongly supporting a particular party, cause, or person; one-sided and prejudiced.
meanings.example: The report was dismissed as partisan by government supporters, who questioned the independence of the committee.

meanings.definition: A member of an irregular military force operating in occupied territory against an occupying force.
meanings.example: Partisan resistance networks supplied intelligence to Allied command throughout the occupation.

meanings.definition: The male head of a family or tribe; a man who is the founder or leading figure of a group.
meanings.example: As the patriarch of a sprawling business dynasty, he was simultaneously revered and feared by those who depended on his goodwill.

meanings.definition: A bishop of the highest rank in the early and Eastern churches.
meanings.example: The patriarch of Constantinople holds a primacy of honour among the Orthodox churches, though not of jurisdiction.

meanings.definition: To cause someone to feel anxious or unsettled; to disturb.
meanings.example: News of the overnight market collapse visibly perturbed even the most experienced members of the trading floor.

meanings.definition: To cause a departure from an expected orbit or trajectory; to disturb the regular motion of.
meanings.example: The gravitational influence of Jupiter is known to perturb the orbits of asteroids in the main belt.

meanings.definition: Of crucial importance in relation to the development or success of something else; central.
meanings.example: The testimony of the forensic accountant proved pivotal to the prosecution's case, establishing a clear chain of financial transfers.

meanings.definition: Seeming reasonable or probable; apparently valid, acceptable, or honest; likely to be believed.
meanings.example: The alibi was plausible on its surface, but inconsistencies emerged when detectives examined the CCTV footage.

meanings.definition: A dangerous, difficult, or otherwise unfortunate situation; a predicament.
meanings.example: The documentary drew international attention to the plight of nomadic herders whose traditional way of life had been devastated by drought.

meanings.definition: To cause a group to divide into opposing factions; to push opinions or groups to extreme positions.
meanings.example: The proposal polarised the academic community, with supporters and opponents each publishing manifestos attacking the other's position.

meanings.definition: In physics, to restrict the vibrations of a transverse wave to a single plane; to give polarity to.
meanings.example: Polarised light can be used to detect internal stresses in transparent materials.

meanings.definition: An exclusive right or privilege belonging to a particular person or group.
meanings.example: The final decision on deployment remained the prerogative of the chief executive, not the board.

meanings.definition: Failing to observe the limits of what is permitted or appropriate; behaving as if one has more importance or authority than one actually does.
meanings.example: It would be presumptuous to assume the committee will approve the budget without seeing the revised business case.

meanings.definition: An attempt to make something that is not the case appear true; a false show of something.
meanings.example: The negotiations continued, but both sides had largely abandoned the pretence of seeking a genuine compromise.

meanings.definition: A claim to have or be something; a profession of purpose or intention.
meanings.example: He made no pretence of neutrality, openly supporting one faction against another from the outset.

meanings.definition: The fact of being primary, pre-eminent, or more important; the condition of being first in order, rank, or importance.
meanings.example: The country had long claimed primacy in regional affairs, a status that newer powers were increasingly challenging.

meanings.definition: To delay or postpone action; to put off doing something, especially out of habitual carelessness or laziness.
meanings.example: She had a tendency to procrastinate when faced with complex emotional decisions, often waiting until circumstances forced a resolution.

meanings.definition: Remarkably or impressively great in extent, size, or degree; extraordinary.
meanings.example: The scientist's prodigious output over a fifty-year career included more than three hundred peer-reviewed papers.

meanings.definition: Very great or intense; having or showing great knowledge or understanding; far-reaching.
meanings.example: The loss had a profound effect on the community, altering not just individual lives but the collective sense of security.

meanings.definition: Situated at or extending to a great depth; very deep.
meanings.example: The submarine descended to a profound depth at which pressure would have crushed a conventional vessel.

meanings.definition: To stimulate or give rise to a reaction or emotion, typically a strong or unwelcome one.
meanings.example: The art exhibition provoked intense debate about the limits of free expression and the public funding of controversial work.

meanings.definition: To deliberately annoy or anger someone; to incite to action.
meanings.example: He seemed to derive pleasure from provoking his colleagues, taking positions he did not hold simply to generate conflict.

meanings.definition: Acting with or showing care and thought for the future; sensible and careful, especially in avoiding potential risks.
meanings.example: Given the uncertainty in the bond market, the fund manager judged it prudent to reduce exposure and increase cash reserves.

meanings.definition: Intended as a punishment; extremely harsh, severe, or burdensome.
meanings.example: The punitive tariffs imposed by the bloc were designed to deter future violations of the trade agreement.

meanings.definition: Bitterness or resentfulness, especially when long-standing; deep-seated, often festering resentment.
meanings.example: Years of rancour between the two branches of the family finally came to a head when the estate was put up for sale.

meanings.definition: A set of reasons or a logical basis for a course of action or a particular belief; a reasoned justification.
meanings.example: The policy document failed to provide a convincing rationale for the proposed changes to the regulatory framework.

meanings.definition: A source of help in a difficult situation; the use of someone or something as a source of help.
meanings.example: Having exhausted all available avenues, the tenants had no recourse but to take the landlord to the housing tribunal.

meanings.definition: Filled with remorse; deeply sorry for a past wrong done.
meanings.example: She appeared genuinely remorseful in court, making a detailed apology to those she had harmed through the fraud.

meanings.definition: The expression of disapproval or disappointment; censure or criticism.
meanings.example: His decision to remain silent attracted reproach from colleagues who felt he should have spoken out earlier.

meanings.definition: To address someone with disapproval or criticism; to reprove.
meanings.example: She reproached herself long after the incident, unable to dismiss the belief that she could have done more.

meanings.definition: A firm decision to do or not to do something; a formal expression of opinion or intention by a legislative body.
meanings.example: The UN Security Council passed a resolution demanding an immediate ceasefire and the withdrawal of foreign forces.

meanings.definition: The action of solving a problem or contentious matter; the result of this process.
meanings.example: The mediator achieved a resolution that, while not fully satisfying either party, prevented further escalation.

meanings.definition: The degree of detail visible in a photographic image or on a display screen.
meanings.example: The telescope's resolution was sufficient to distinguish individual craters less than a kilometre across.

meanings.definition: To examine or inspect closely and thoroughly; to investigate or review in detail.
meanings.example: Auditors were brought in to scrutinise the accounts after several irregularities came to light during the quarterly review.

meanings.definition: Of or prompted by feelings of tenderness, sadness, or nostalgia; easily affected by emotion.
meanings.example: He had kept the battered notebook for decades, more for sentimental reasons than any practical utility.

meanings.definition: Excessively nostalgic or emotional in a way that seems shallow or insincere.
meanings.example: Critics dismissed the film as cloying and sentimental, arguing that it resolved its dark subject matter too neatly.

meanings.definition: The occurrence and development of events by chance in a happy or beneficial way; a fortunate accident.
meanings.example: The discovery of penicillin is often cited as a classic case of serendipity, arising from a contaminated Petri dish.

meanings.definition: Having or showing sharp powers of judgement; clever or astute, especially in practical matters.
meanings.example: A shrewd investor, she had moved the bulk of her portfolio out of equities six months before the crash.
